symptoms: Water-soaked yellow-to-white stripes on leaf margins that turn yellow-brown; leaves wilt and dry from the tip; in seedlings whole plant may wilt (kresek stage).
causes: Bacteria spreads via irrigation water, rain splash, wind and contaminated seed/tools; favored by warm humid weather (25-34°C) and high nitrogen.
visual_detection_cues: Long wavy yellow lesions running parallel to leaf veins starting at margins/tip, later turning grayish-white with wavy edge.
cure_treatment: Drain field and avoid flooding; spray Copper oxychloride (0.3%) or Streptocycline (100-200 ppm) mixed with Copper oxychloride; use resistant varieties for next season.
prevention_suggestions: Use certified disease-free seed; balanced NPK (avoid excess nitrogen); maintain field drainage; avoid clipping seedling leaf tips during transplanting; rotate with non-host crop.

symptoms: Diamond/spindle shaped grey-centered spots with brown margins on leaves; node blast causes blackening and breaking of stem; neck blast causes empty panicles.
causes: High humidity, extended leaf wetness, dense planting, excess nitrogen, temperature 25-28°C.
visual_detection_cues: Eye/diamond-shaped lesions with grey/white center and reddish-brown border on leaf blade; blackened node or neck.
cure_treatment: Spray Tricyclazole 75% WP (0.6 g/L) or Isoprothiolane at first symptom; repeat after 10-15 days if needed.
prevention_suggestions: Use resistant varieties; avoid excess nitrogen; ensure proper spacing for airflow; treat seed with Carbendazim before sowing.

symptoms: Oval greenish-grey water-soaked lesions on leaf sheath near water line, enlarging with irregular brown margins; lesions may girdle the sheath.
causes: High humidity, dense canopy, excess nitrogen, standing water, temperature 28-32°C.
visual_detection_cues: Cloud/snake-skin pattern lesions on lower leaf sheath, greyish-white center with brown border.
cure_treatment: Spray Hexaconazole 5% SC or Validamycin at early tillering/booting stage; drain excess water.
prevention_suggestions: Avoid overcrowding, maintain optimum spacing, balanced fertilization, remove infected stubble after harvest.

symptoms: Small circular to oval brown spots with grey/white center on leaves and grain; severe infection causes leaf blight.
causes: Nutrient-deficient soil (especially potassium), high humidity, stressed/old seedlings.
visual_detection_cues: Numerous small round dark-brown spots scattered across leaf surface, resembling sesame seeds.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb 0.2% or Propiconazole; apply potash fertilizer to correct deficiency.
prevention_suggestions: Use healthy seed, treat seed with Carbendazim, maintain balanced soil fertility (esp. potassium & zinc).

symptoms: Yellow-orange powdery pustules arranged in stripes along leaf veins; severe infection causes leaf drying and yield loss.
causes: Cool moist weather (10-15°C), high humidity, dew, wind-borne spores.
visual_detection_cues: Bright yellow powdery streaks in linear rows along the leaf blade, rub off as yellow powder on finger.
cure_treatment: Spray Propiconazole 25% EC (0.1%) or Tebuconazole at first appearance; repeat after 15 days if severe.
prevention_suggestions: Grow resistant varieties, timely sowing, avoid excess nitrogen, monitor field regularly during cool season.

symptoms: Small round orange-brown pustules scattered irregularly (not in stripes) on upper leaf surface.
causes: Warm humid weather (15-25°C), dense crop canopy, wind dispersal of spores.
visual_detection_cues: Round orange-brown powdery pustules randomly scattered on leaf, distinguishing it from the striped yellow rust.
cure_treatment: Spray Propiconazole or Mancozeb at first sign; two sprays 15 days apart usually sufficient.
prevention_suggestions: Plant resistant varieties, avoid late sowing, balanced fertilizer application.

symptoms: White powdery fungal growth on leaves, stems and ear; leaves turn yellow and wither in severe cases.
causes: Cool humid weather, dense sowing, shade, excess nitrogen.
visual_detection_cues: Talcum-powder-like white patches on upper leaf surface, easily wiped off.
cure_treatment: Spray Sulphur dust (25kg/ha) or wettable Sulphur 0.2%, or Propiconazole.
prevention_suggestions: Avoid dense sowing, use resistant varieties, avoid excess irrigation/nitrogen.

symptoms: Ear head replaced by black powdery mass of spores, visible at heading stage; whole grain destroyed.
causes: Seed-borne infection, spores enter floral parts during flowering of previous crop.
visual_detection_cues: Black soot-like powder covering the entire ear head instead of grains.
cure_treatment: No in-season cure; rogue out and destroy infected ears before spores spread; treat seed for next season.
prevention_suggestions: Use certified disease-free seed; treat seed with Carboxin or Vitavax (2-2.5 g/kg seed) before sowing.

disease_name: Turcicum Leaf Blight (Northern Corn Leaf Blight)
symptoms: Long elliptical greyish-green to tan lesions on leaves, running parallel to leaf margin; lesions coalesce causing leaf blight.
causes: Warm humid weather, continuous cropping, crop residue carrying spores.
visual_detection_cues: Cigar-shaped grey-tan lesions, 2-15 cm long, on lower leaves first.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP (0.25%) or Propiconazole at early symptom stage; repeat after 10-15 days.
prevention_suggestions: Use resistant hybrids, crop rotation, destroy crop residue, avoid dense planting.

symptoms: Small round to elongated reddish-brown pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces.
causes: Cool moist weather (16-23°C), high humidity, wind-borne spores.
visual_detection_cues: Cinnamon-brown powdery pustules on both sides of the leaf, rupturing the epidermis.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb or Propiconazole if severity is high before tasseling.
prevention_suggestions: Plant resistant hybrids, avoid late planting, monitor fields regularly.

symptoms: Ragged/window-pane feeding on leaves, holes in whorl, sawdust-like frass (excreta) in the funnel.
causes: Moth lays eggs on leaves; larvae feed voraciously especially in whorl stage; favored by warm weather.
visual_detection_cues: Skeletonized leaves with round-to-oblong holes in a row pattern; larvae with inverted 'Y' mark on head visible in whorl.
cure_treatment: Apply Emamectin benzoate 5% SG (0.4 g/L) or Spinetoram directly into the whorl; hand-pick larvae in small fields.
prevention_suggestions: Early sowing, regular scouting, use pheromone traps, encourage natural predators, intercrop with legumes.

symptoms: Small angular water-soaked spots on leaves bound by veins, turning brown/black; black lesions on stems and bolls.
causes: Rain splash, wind, contaminated seed, warm humid weather.
visual_detection_cues: Angular dark spots restricted by leaf veins (not round), giving a mosaic-like pattern.
cure_treatment: Spray Copper oxychloride (0.3%) with Streptocycline (100 ppm); remove and destroy infected plant debris.
prevention_suggestions: Use acid-delinted/treated seed, avoid overhead irrigation, crop rotation with non-host crops.

symptoms: Rosette flowers (petals stuck together); bolls have small entry holes, damaged/pink-stained lint and seeds inside.
causes: Moth lays eggs on squares/bolls; larvae bore inside, favored by continuous cotton cultivation and warm weather.
visual_detection_cues: Rosetted flower petals; small dark holes on boll surface with frass; pink caterpillar found inside boll on cutting open.
cure_treatment: Spray Profenofos 50% EC or use pheromone (gossyplure) traps; apply Emamectin benzoate if infestation is high.
prevention_suggestions: Install pheromone traps early season, destroy crop residue after harvest, avoid ratoon cotton, timely sowing and uprooting after last picking.

scientific_name: Cotton leaf curl virus (CLCuV), transmitted by whitefly
symptoms: Upward/downward curling of leaves, thickened veins, leaf-like enations on the underside, stunted growth and reduced boll formation.
causes: Spread by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) vector; favored by hot dry weather that boosts whitefly population.
visual_detection_cues: Leaves curled and puckered with dark green thickened veins and small leafy outgrowths (enations) on the underside.
cure_treatment: No direct cure; control whitefly vector with Imidacloprid or Diafenthiuron sprays; remove and destroy infected plants early.
prevention_suggestions: Grow tolerant varieties, install yellow sticky traps for whitefly monitoring, avoid overlapping cotton seasons.

symptoms: Dark brown concentric-ringed (target-board) spots on older/lower leaves first, leading to yellowing and defoliation; can also affect stem and fruit.
causes: Warm humid weather, alternating wet-dry conditions, nutrient-stressed plants.
visual_detection_cues: Circular brown spots with visible concentric rings like a target/bullseye, surrounded by yellow halo.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP (0.25%) or Chlorothalonil at 10-day intervals; remove infected lower leaves.
prevention_suggestions: Stake plants for airflow, mulch to prevent soil splash, crop rotation, avoid overhead watering.

symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked greyish-green patches on leaves that turn brown/black rapidly; white fungal growth on underside in humid weather; fruit develops brown greasy patches.
causes: Cool wet weather (10-20°C), high humidity, poor air circulation.
visual_detection_cues: Water-soaked dark patches on leaves with white cottony growth on the underside margin, spreading rapidly within days.
cure_treatment: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil) or Cymoxanil immediately at first sign; act fast as it spreads quickly.
prevention_suggestions: Ensure good drainage/airflow, avoid overhead irrigation especially evenings, remove and burn infected plants immediately.

scientific_name: Tomato leaf curl virus, transmitted by whitefly
symptoms: Upward curling and crinkling of leaves, yellowing between veins, stunted bushy growth, flower drop and poor fruit set.
causes: Spread by whitefly vector; hot dry weather favors whitefly buildup.
visual_detection_cues: Small, curled, crinkled leaves giving the plant a stunted, bushy appearance; yellow mottling between veins.
cure_treatment: No cure once infected — remove and destroy infected plants; control whitefly with Imidacloprid or yellow sticky traps.
prevention_suggestions: Use virus-resistant varieties, raise seedlings under insect-proof nylon net, control whitefly early.

symptoms: Numerous small circular spots with dark margin and grey/tan center, mainly on older leaves, causing yellowing and leaf drop.
causes: Warm humid weather, overhead watering, splashing rain.
visual_detection_cues: Small (2-3mm) round spots with dark brown border and light grey center, tiny black specks (pycnidia) visible at center.
cure_treatment: Spray Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb every 7-10 days; remove infected leaves.
prevention_suggestions: Avoid overhead irrigation, mulch soil, rotate crops, space plants for airflow.

symptoms: Dark water-soaked patches on leaves and stem that enlarge rapidly, white mold on leaf underside in humid conditions; tubers show reddish-brown rot.
causes: Cool wet weather, high humidity, poor drainage.
visual_detection_cues: Irregular dark brown-black lesions with pale green border on leaves, white downy growth visible on underside in early morning.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb preventively; Metalaxyl+Mancozeb or Cymoxanil+Mancozeb curatively at first symptom.
prevention_suggestions: Use certified disease-free seed tubers, earth-up soil around plants, avoid excess irrigation, destroy infected haulms before harvest.

symptoms: Dark brown target-board spots with concentric rings on older leaves, causing yellowing and premature defoliation.
causes: Warm weather with alternating dry-wet spells, plant stress, low nitrogen.
visual_detection_cues: Circular brown lesions with concentric ring pattern, primarily on lower/older leaves.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil at 10-day intervals.
prevention_suggestions: Balanced fertilization, crop rotation, remove volunteer potato plants.

symptoms: Rough, corky, raised or pitted brown lesions on tuber skin; does not usually affect foliage.
causes: Alkaline/dry soil, low soil moisture during tuber formation, high soil pH.
visual_detection_cues: Rough corky brown scab-like patches on the tuber surface (visible after harvest, not on standing crop leaves).
cure_treatment: No cure for existing tubers; maintain consistent soil moisture during tuber bulking; lower soil pH if needed.
prevention_suggestions: Use resistant varieties, avoid excess lime/alkaline amendments, maintain even irrigation, rotate crops.

symptoms: Reddening of internal stalk tissue with white patches (cross-section), drying of leaves, sour smell from split cane.
causes: Waterlogging, use of infected setts, warm humid weather.
visual_detection_cues: On cutting the stalk, red discoloration with white transverse bands is visible inside; leaves show drying/wilting from top.
cure_treatment: No effective in-field cure; remove and burn infected clumps; treat healthy setts with Carbendazim before planting next crop.
prevention_suggestions: Use disease-free/resistant sett varieties, hot water treatment of setts (50°C for 2 hrs), avoid waterlogging, crop rotation.

symptoms: Long black whip-like structure emerging from the growing point (shoot converts into a curved black whip covered with spores).
causes: Airborne spores, infected setts, favored by drought stress and ratooning.
visual_detection_cues: Distinctive black curved whip protruding from the top of the cane, covered in powdery black spore mass.
cure_treatment: Rogue out and burn whip-bearing clumps immediately before whip ruptures; treat setts with Propiconazole or Carbendazim before planting.
prevention_suggestions: Use resistant varieties, avoid ratooning infected fields, hot water treatment of setts.

symptoms: Sunken circular dark lesions with concentric rings on ripe fruit, often with pinkish spore masses in center; fruit shrivels and drops.
causes: Warm humid weather, rain splash, infected seed.
visual_detection_cues: Sunken dark, water-soaked circular spots on the fruit with visible concentric rings and pink/orange spore dots at center.
cure_treatment: Spray Carbendazim + Mancozeb (combi) or Copper oxychloride at 10-day intervals starting from flowering.
prevention_suggestions: Use disease-free seed, avoid overhead irrigation, remove and destroy infected fruit, crop rotation.

scientific_name: Chilli leaf curl virus, transmitted by whitefly
symptoms: Upward curling, crinkling and thickening of leaves; stunted plant with bushy top, reduced flowering and fruiting.
causes: Whitefly vector transmission, hot dry weather.
visual_detection_cues: Small, tightly curled, leathery leaves clustered at the top of the plant giving a bushy/stunted look.
cure_treatment: No cure — remove and destroy infected plants; control whitefly with Imidacloprid or Diafenthiuron sprays.
prevention_suggestions: Grow tolerant varieties, use yellow sticky traps, raise nursery under insect-proof net, avoid planting near old infected fields.

symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf surface with white powdery fungal growth on the corresponding underside; leaves drop prematurely.
causes: Warm dry days with cool humid nights, poor air circulation.
visual_detection_cues: White powdery coating mainly on leaf underside, with yellow blotches visible on the upper surface.
cure_treatment: Spray wettable Sulphur (0.2%) or Hexaconazole.
prevention_suggestions: Ensure good spacing/airflow, avoid water stress, remove heavily infected leaves.

symptoms: Small water-soaked white lesions that enlarge into purplish-brown concentric-ringed patches on leaves and seed stalks, causing leaf collapse.
causes: Warm humid weather, overhead irrigation, dense planting.
visual_detection_cues: Elongated lesions with purple-brown center and yellow halo, concentric zonation visible, leaf tip drying and drooping.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP (0.25%) or Iprodione at 10-day intervals.
prevention_suggestions: Avoid overhead irrigation, ensure field drainage, crop rotation, wider plant spacing.

symptoms: Pale yellow-green oval patches on leaves with greyish-purple fungal fuzz in humid mornings; leaves collapse and dry from tip.
causes: Cool humid weather, heavy dew, poor drainage.
visual_detection_cues: Grey-purple fuzzy mold visible on leaf surface in early morning, leaves pale and bent over.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb or Metalaxyl+Mancozeb at first appearance.
prevention_suggestions: Use disease-free sets/seed, improve field drainage and airflow, avoid excess irrigation.

symptoms: Small circular brown-black spots with yellow halo on leaves, leading to defoliation in severe cases.
causes: Warm humid weather, continuous groundnut cultivation, rain splash.
visual_detection_cues: Round dark brown to black spots (early spot lighter brown, late spot darker/black) each with a yellow ring around it.
cure_treatment: Spray Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb (0.2%) at 15-day intervals starting 30 days after sowing.
prevention_suggestions: Crop rotation, use resistant varieties, remove crop debris after harvest, timely sowing.

symptoms: Rotting and blackening at the collar/stem base near soil line, seedling wilts and topples over.
causes: Infected seed, high soil temperature, poor seed quality.
visual_detection_cues: Black sooty fungal growth at the base of stem near soil surface, wilting seedlings that snap easily at collar region.
cure_treatment: Treat seed with Carbendazim/Thiram before sowing; drench soil with Carbendazim if detected early.
prevention_suggestions: Use certified treated seed, avoid deep sowing, maintain field drainage.

scientific_name: Mungbean yellow mosaic virus, transmitted by whitefly
symptoms: Bright yellow mosaic patches on leaves, stunted plant growth, reduced pod formation and small distorted pods.
causes: Whitefly vector transmission, favored by warm weather and whitefly buildup.
visual_detection_cues: Irregular bright yellow patches mixed with green on leaves in a mosaic pattern.
cure_treatment: No cure — rogue out infected plants early; control whitefly with Imidacloprid.
prevention_suggestions: Grow tolerant varieties, early sowing to avoid peak whitefly season, use yellow sticky traps.

symptoms: Small reddish-brown to tan pustules mainly on leaf underside, leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely.
causes: Warm humid weather, wind-borne spores, dense canopy.
visual_detection_cues: Tiny raised tan-brown pustules on the underside of leaves, gritty texture when rubbed.
cure_treatment: Spray Hexaconazole or Tebuconazole at first sign, repeat after 15 days if needed.
prevention_suggestions: Use resistant varieties, avoid late sowing, monitor fields during flowering/pod stage.

symptoms: Yellowing of older leaves starting from margin, leaves collapse and hang around pseudostem, internal stem shows dark brown vascular discoloration.
causes: Soil-borne fungus, spreads through infected suckers/soil/water, persists in soil for years.
visual_detection_cues: Yellowing progressing from oldest to youngest leaves, split pseudostem shows dark brown-black streaks inside.
cure_treatment: No cure — remove and destroy infected plants including rhizome; do not replant banana in same spot for years.
prevention_suggestions: Use disease-free tissue-culture plantlets, avoid waterlogging, practice long crop rotation, disinfect tools.

symptoms: Small yellow streaks that enlarge into brown-black oval spots with yellow halo, coalescing to cause leaf death.
causes: Warm humid weather, dense planting, poor drainage.
visual_detection_cues: Parallel brown-black streaks/spots running along leaf veins that merge into large necrotic patches.
cure_treatment: Spray Propiconazole or Mancozeb at 15-day intervals; remove and destroy heavily infected leaves.
prevention_suggestions: Maintain plant spacing for airflow, remove old infected leaves regularly, balanced fertilization, improve drainage.

symptoms: Pale yellow oily patches on upper leaf surface, white cottony fungal growth on the underside, affected berries shrivel (brown rot).
causes: Cool wet weather, high humidity, overhead irrigation, poor canopy airflow.
visual_detection_cues: Oily translucent yellow spots on top of leaf with white downy fungal growth visible underneath.
cure_treatment: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb or Copper oxychloride at first sign, repeat after rains.
prevention_suggestions: Canopy management for airflow, avoid overhead irrigation, timely pruning, remove infected leaves/berries.

symptoms: White powdery fungal growth on leaves, shoots and berries; infected berries crack and split.
causes: Warm dry days with high humidity at night, dense canopy.
visual_detection_cues: Talcum-like white powder coating leaves, shoots and young berries.
cure_treatment: Spray wettable Sulphur (0.2%) or Hexaconazole/Myclobutanil.
prevention_suggestions: Canopy management, avoid excess nitrogen, timely sulphur dusting during susceptible stage.

symptoms: Black irregular spots on leaves, flowers (blossom blight causing flower drop) and fruit (sunken black spots that enlarge in storage).
causes: Warm humid weather, rain during flowering, poor orchard sanitation.
visual_detection_cues: Irregular black-brown spots on leaves/fruit with slightly sunken centers, black shriveled flower panicles (blossom blight).
cure_treatment: Spray Carbendazim (0.1%) or Copper oxychloride at flowering and again at fruit development stage.
prevention_suggestions: Prune for airflow, remove fallen leaves/fruit, avoid overhead irrigation during flowering.

symptoms: White powdery growth on flower panicles, young leaves and fruit; causes flower and fruit drop.
causes: Cool dry weather with high humidity at night during flowering season.
visual_detection_cues: White powdery coating on inflorescence/panicle giving a frosted appearance.
cure_treatment: Spray wettable Sulphur (0.2%) or Hexaconazole at panicle emergence, repeat at 15-day intervals.
prevention_suggestions: Monitor during flowering season, prune for airflow, avoid dense canopy.

symptoms: Raised corky brown lesions with yellow halo on leaves, stems and fruit; leaves may drop, fruit gets blemished.
causes: Rain splash, wind-driven rain, contaminated pruning tools, leaf miner injury sites.
visual_detection_cues: Rough, raised, corky brown circular lesions with a water-soaked yellow ring around them on leaves and fruit rind.
cure_treatment: Spray Copper oxychloride (0.3%) or Streptocycline; prune and burn infected twigs.
prevention_suggestions: Control citrus leaf miner (entry point for bacteria), use windbreaks, disinfect pruning tools between plants.

scientific_name: Candidatus Liberibacter spp., transmitted by psyllid
symptoms: Blotchy yellow mottling of leaves (asymmetric across midrib), stunted growth, small lopsided bitter fruit that stays green at base.
causes: Spread by Asian citrus psyllid; no cure once infected.
visual_detection_cues: Yellow blotchy mottled pattern on leaves that is asymmetric on either side of midrib (distinguishes from nutrient deficiency).
cure_treatment: No cure — remove and destroy infected trees to prevent spread; control psyllid vector with Imidacloprid.
prevention_suggestions: Use certified disease-free nursery plants, monitor and control psyllid population, remove infected trees promptly.

symptoms: Olive-green to black velvety spots on leaves and fruit; fruit develops corky, cracked scab-like lesions.
causes: Cool wet spring weather, overwintering spores on fallen leaves.
visual_detection_cues: Dark olive-green to black scabby spots with feathery/velvety edges on leaf surface and fruit skin.
cure_treatment: Spray Mancozeb or Captan at bud break, repeat at 10-day intervals through wet spring.
prevention_suggestions: Remove and destroy fallen leaves in autumn, prune for airflow, use resistant varieties.

symptoms: White powdery coating on young leaves, shoots and blossoms; leaves curl and shoots become stunted.
causes: Warm dry weather, high humidity, infected buds overwintering the fungus.
visual_detection_cues: White felty powder on new shoot tips and leaf undersides, curled/distorted young leaves.
cure_treatment: Spray wettable Sulphur or Myclobutanil at bud break, repeat during shoot growth.
prevention_suggestions: Prune out infected shoots in dormant season, avoid excess nitrogen, ensure airflow.

symptoms: Wilting of shoots (dead heart) due to larval boring; small entry holes on fruit plugged with excreta, fruit rots internally.
causes: Moth lays eggs on shoots/fruit; larvae bore in, favored by warm humid weather and continuous cropping.
visual_detection_cues: Wilted drooping shoot tips; round holes with frass plugging on fruit surface, larva visible inside on cutting fruit.
cure_treatment: Remove and destroy wilted shoots/infested fruit; spray Emamectin benzoate or Spinosad at early infestation.
prevention_suggestions: Install pheromone traps, clip and destroy affected shoots weekly, avoid continuous brinjal cropping in same field.

symptoms: Sudden wilting of whole plant despite moist soil; cut stem oozes milky bacterial ooze in water test.
causes: Soil-borne bacteria, warm wet soil, spreads via irrigation water and infected soil/tools.
visual_detection_cues: Rapid wilting of entire plant while still green; milky white ooze from cut stem placed in clear water (diagnostic test).
cure_treatment: No cure — remove and destroy wilted plants immediately; drench soil around healthy plants with Copper oxychloride/Bleaching powder.
prevention_suggestions: Crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops for 2-3 years, use resistant rootstock/grafted seedlings, improve drainage.

symptoms: Uniform yellowing (chlorosis) starting from older/lower leaves, stunted growth, thin weak stems.
causes: Sandy/leached soil, insufficient fertilization, heavy rainfall washing away nitrogen.
visual_detection_cues: Overall pale yellow-green color of older leaves first, no spots or lesions present (helps distinguish from disease).
cure_treatment: Apply Urea or ammonium sulphate as top dressing per crop recommendation; foliar spray of 2% urea for quick recovery.
prevention_suggestions: Soil testing before sowing, split nitrogen application, add organic manure/compost.

symptoms: Yellowing and scorching (browning) of leaf margins/tips on older leaves, weak stalks, poor grain/fruit filling.
causes: Potassium-poor soil, imbalanced fertilization, sandy soils with high leaching.
visual_detection_cues: Brown scorched/burnt appearance restricted to leaf edges while the leaf center remains green.
cure_treatment: Apply Muriate of Potash (MOP) as per soil test recommendation.
prevention_suggestions: Regular soil testing, balanced NPK fertilization, use potash-rich organic manure.

symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis (yellowing between green veins) on young leaves, small/narrow leaves, stunted internodes (rosetting).
causes: Alkaline/calcareous soils, excess phosphorus fertilization, sandy low-organic-matter soils.
visual_detection_cues: Distinct green veins standing out against pale yellow leaf tissue between them, mainly on newer growth.
cure_treatment: Foliar spray of Zinc sulphate (0.5%) or soil application per recommendation.
prevention_suggestions: Soil test and apply zinc sulphate before sowing in deficient soils, add organic matter/compost.
